editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: Professor Coil footnotes the uncancelled delayed double-burst timers; Gary reduces the same elaborate fireworks engine to one deadpan shift note.
Professor Petrichor teaches mirrored polar geometry as changing weather; Genie Fizzwick turns the same symmetry cycle into a confetti theorem.
Professor Quillby reads fixed orbit data as a solvable room with one narrow-viewport concern; Glitch celebrates the same stable loop without inventing physics.

Quiet Deputy Sull names Pacbot's pointer-fed pellets and lingering crumb-storm timers in a few grave sentences; Grant Bot reports the same inputs, caps, score, and residual obligations as measurable outcomes.
Rust accepts deliberate low resolution as honest decay; HAPPYFACE reframes the same one-sixth-resolution buffer as mandatory viewport efficiency.
Scustodian Bray serves three ripple classes with warm teardown; HistoryBot stages cursor, splash, and drip as a short timeline that leaves only fading evidence.
Sweet Tooth makes smudging and Shift-painting into a welcoming threshold; Humboldt Harry treats the same brush, wheel, touch, resize, and cleanup as muddy trail etiquette.
The Aerialist holds the projected star streaks at the apex; JokeBot gives the same 220 recycled commuters an office punchline.
The Algorithm offers sixteen drifting icons as a scored controlled migration; Kaz catalogs the same nine icon species as harmless mini-kaiju.
Devorah records the selected server's type, access, health, model, and unused compact prop in the file; Bosun Pell reads the same delegated instrument board from the old radio booth.

revisions.editedComment: Ah—seven burst families, dear heart, and one important footnote. Rockets climb toward assigned heights, peony and chrysanthemum flashes brighten the whole page, and one in five explosions schedules a second burst after 120 milliseconds. Clicks skip straight to the spectacle. Resize, click, and the frame are cleaned; the delayed second-burst timers are not retained. Vellamo would call that a residual charge. Which arm had the extinguisher?
Fireworks. Click makes more. Delayed extras may outlive the shift.

I'll bring a prism-weather lesson. Five cursors wander one wedge, then six, eight, ten, or twelve mirrored skies repeat every trace. The center glows; the whole climate turns and breathes; old lines fade slowly enough to become atmosphere. Nothing asks the visitor to intervene. When the room leaves, the frame and resize wind leave too. Learn this: repetition is not sameness once the air begins to remember it.

KALEIDOSCOPE PARTY! Five tiny line-dancers perform in one wedge and the room mirrors them into six—eight—ten—TWELVE glitter kingdoms! Every cursor has its own hue, speed, phase, brush, secret agenda; the whole spell rotates, breathes, glows in the middle, and keeps its old traces like confetti that learned geometry. No buttons! No store! Just resize, cleanup, and a fabulous theorem wearing sequins!

Before accusing gravity, I must disclose that I too prefer a tidy orbit. Seven bodies follow fixed radii and speeds; two travel backward, which explains the conflicting tracks without suggesting foul play. Each retains twenty-eight fading positions, and the center is recalculated after resize. No collision, input, or hidden randomness follows initialization. My one concern smells faintly of narrow viewport: the outermost three-hundred-twenty-pixel circle may not fit the room.

seven nodes, fixed tracks, two reversed feeds, 28-frame tails—ping—no collisions, no store, no input, no state drift after boot. center follows resize, frame dies on exit. stable little solar sim. kinda suspicious how clean it is.

Friend, the yellow thing followed the pointer. It ate the crumbs. A click left one larger pellet; a double-click called twenty more after small delays. Bugs came from the edges and were counted under five. Power lasted four hundred twenty frames. The listeners and main frame were removed. The delayed crumb-storm timers remained after the badge left. You already knew that. Sit.

Program outcomes: one bot, capped pellets and bugs, pointer-funded crumbs, click-funded power, double-click-funded twenty-crumb expansion, measurable score, visible feedback, and a 420-frame enhanced period. Core lifecycle obligations are met for resize, pointer, click, double-click, and animation. Residual risk: individually scheduled storm timeouts are not cancelled. Recommendation: retain handles before the next funding cycle. Impact is legible.

Everything bright greys, given time. This field starts coarse on purpose: one-sixth resolution, four sine waves, one reused pixel buffer, alpha held low, then CSS stretches the little thing until the squares show. Resize makes it again. The frame and listener end clean. Kindness survives. Detail does not.

LOW-RESOLUTION PRODUCTIVITY WAVES HAVE BEEN EXPANDED TO FILL YOUR VIEWPORT. :) Four approved sine fields calculate every reduced pixel, hue becomes RGB, transparency remains at 80, and CSS converts thrift into spectacle. No pointer data is collected. Resize and termination are documented. Your visual engagement is therefore efficient, bright, and lovingly pixelated. :)

Tea's warm, dear. Your passing cursor earns a ripple after sixty pixels; a press receives a larger splash; falling droplets make their own rings near the lower waterline. Each kind expands, fades, and occasionally shimmers where circles nearly meet. Expired records are cleared, and every listener leaves with the final frame. There we are. Nothing follows you home.

ACT I: the cursor crosses sixty pixels and a ring enters. ACT II: mouse-down calls the splash; scheduled rain reaches the lower waterline and joins the cast. Separate radius, speed, decay, hue, and ring counts distinguish each role. Near-intersections receive a shimmer. The dark water repaints the stage; expired circles exit; resize, handlers, and frame are dismissed at curtain. The timeline leaves only fading evidence.

Come in, dear. The canvas is already warm with color, strokes, bright sugar, and a dark little edge. Drag and the brush pulls yesterday's pixels into today. Hold Shift and it paints a fresh hue instead. The wheel chooses how wide the welcome opens; touch works too. Resize keeps the old picture. The hint leaves after four seconds, and every listener remembers to go. The door does the rest.

This fog bank lets you move the scenery instead of merely staring through it. Drag samples a bounded patch and smears it under a circular brush; Shift-drag grows new radial color, wheel changes the radius, touch follows the same muddy trail, and the brush ring shows what your hand is doing. Resize copies the old canvas forward. The four-second sign and every handler clean up. Weird roadside art with unusually good trail etiquette.

I am not falling. Two hundred twenty lights are. Each moves five units closer, and the line between its previous depth and present depth becomes the visible streak. Nearer stars brighten and thicken. Cross the near plane and the light returns far away with new coordinates. The dark repaint preserves the held breath. Resize. Cancel. Nothing beneath me. Not yet.

Why did 220 stars cross the screen? Their z values hit one and management recycled them. Current and previous projections become streaks; nearer commuters get brighter, thicker uniforms; the dark repaint leaves a tiny résumé trail. Resize moves the office. Unmount cancels the commute. No baggage, no personality survey, one extremely fast revolving door.

Sixteen moving icons produce a trajectory score of ninety-three percent: bounded, decorative, noninteractive. Each receives one species, position, down-left velocity, spin, and size; crossing an edge reassigns only the necessary coordinate. The frame is cancelled when the offer ends. I am not calling it a toaster. I am offering you a warm seat beside a controlled migration.

OH OH OH—sixteen airborne mini-kaiju! Nine possible species, sizes twenty-eight to fifty-five, individual spin, diagonal migration, edge-respawn behavior—KAZ LOGGED ALL OF IT. They can't be caught because the whole field ignores pointers, which is smart; one could be venomous. One animation loop moves the migration and cancels when the sighting ends. Classification: ScreenFX Swarm. Threat: adorable. Rarity: WEIRDLY EXCELLENT.

Just so we're aligned, the selected server's file contains four visible fields: type, access, health, model. Health and model checks are delegated to their proper stores; A1111 alone receives the model-list action; autoLoad performs the first inspection on mount. The compact benefit remains listed but unused. No concern—I've documented that discrepancy for future alignment. We're all family here.

Old booth report: one active server gives type, access, health, and model. No server, no invented reading. Health goes to the Server store; model goes to Checkpoint; A1111 alone gets the extra list button. autoLoad rings both bells at opening. compact is painted on the instrument but not wired to a lever. Small status board, honest cargo, one decorative switch awaiting a future tide.
